summary: Gruber examines the complex triangular relationship between Apple, Google, and Palm regarding multi-touch patents and mobile competition. He corroborates a report that Google showed Apple a pre-release G1 prototype to avoid patent disputes, and that Apple asked Google not to implement multi-touch in Android. He contrasts Google's cooperative relationship with Apple against Palm's increasingly hostile one, noting Palm's aggressive poaching of Apple engineers. The piece argues that while Google and Apple share a kinship rooted in complementary business models and a common rival in Microsoft, Palm's direct competition in handheld hardware makes its relationship with Apple frigid.
key_points: Google reportedly showed Apple a pre-release HTC G1 prototype to avoid patent disputes, and Apple objected to both multi-touch and the standard headphone jack
Apple apparently owns a patent on software control via buttons connected through a standard 3.5mm headphone jack, which forced the G1 to use a proprietary ExtUSB port
Google and Apple compete very little overall — Apple sells hardware, Google sells search advertising — making Android vs. iPhone OS the biggest exception
Palm's aggressive recruitment of Apple engineers at 1.5x salary and its direct competition in handheld hardware have made the Apple-Palm relationship ice cold
Google's cooperative posture toward Apple contrasts sharply with Palm's confrontational approach on multi-touch patents
money_quotes: You know there's no way that Apple is showing pre-release iPhone hardware or software to Google — so why would Google do this?
Google and Apple do have a sort of kinship. They compete very little overall — Apple makes money by selling computing hardware products; Google makes money through search advertising.
Unlike Siegler, I think the large number of recent Apple employees now working at Palm on the Pre suggests that the relationship between the two companies is cold — ice cold.
What I heard last month at Macworld Expo is that Palm has a standing offer for engineers at Apple to jump ship, with a starting salary of 1.5 times their current Apple salary.
I wouldn't hold my breath waiting for Palm CEO Ed Colligan or former Apple senior VP Jon Rubinstein to get a seat on Apple's board.
Google's own iPhone apps, like the Google Search app, have a decidedly Google-ish aesthetic.
key_insight: Google's willingness to defer to Apple on patents like multi-touch reveals a cooperative relationship built on complementary business models, while Palm's direct competition and aggressive poaching of Apple talent has made that relationship hostile.
